content:
In the RFL, saw 

/// # Guarantees
// SAFETY:
/// # Safety
/// # Invariants
// INVARIANT

Can we have some doc guide for this?
This is mine understanding:
In the algo area, to ensure algo working correctly, the algo need meet those properties: safety, liveness, fairness. As for our rust view, maybe the memory saftety/thread safety is about the safety property for our coded code, such as code segment for operating some var, a code block, or the whole algo code, but we only focus low level the memory/thread safety, not the algo's self safety.
For example, for mutex algo, it is (high level) safety is that : At most one process at a time executes the critical section.
As for invariant, i fount it is just one class safety of a algo. Should we should always use the safety?
The Guarantees look like also denote the safety.

To understanding the algo/rust code, we indeed need to distinguish two idea: safety we need the aglo/code should have, safety the code currently have impled, or user provide. The first is about aim, the second is about impl detail.
When look at the rust code in RFL, i alway confuse with the difference.

So can we supply one guide or add some explanation or improve our comment.

You're welcome to submit a patch if you think this file can be improved:

https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/Documentation/rust/coding-guidelines.rst
although I think that doc answers/resolves your question here already.

the doc guide look like say: 

/// # Safetye       == >aim/safety requirement
// SAFETY      ==> impl to achieve the safety, which detail how /why the impl  satisfying the safety.

Yes, this clear some confuse.
As for Guarantees, invariant, look like those not been mentioned by the doc.
I wanted to create a guide/standard for writing safety comments, but have been occupied with other things. since Kangrejos is now over, I will probably pick it up again soon-ish
:+1: Now i wait you
